So Walmart's whole business, particularly their superstores that have food, is about people buying things they don't need on impulse to feel a little better about their circumstances when they come to buy the basics. That's their whole business. The only profit is in the DVD and the toy and the extra kitchen organizer and that that people buy when they come to get toilet paper and ground meat and four cans of soup and they spend an extra half an hour in the store because that's free entertainment and they buy four or five things and they feel better about them. They don't need any of them. Most people, even them who buy a DVD, have DVDs at home they haven't watched yet. Still a rapper. I mean, but it's still a rapper. They don't need it. Plus they got a television. And they got Netflix for Christ's sake and they're getting a DVD out of Red Box on the way out to talk. So the no need issue is really a failure of salesmanship. I mean, that's really what it is because most people buy enormous quantities and diversity of things that they do not actually need. Well, and the truth is people buy what they want, not necessarily what they need. So I mean, talking about, you know, I keep buying mentalism books. Well, I mean, that's collecting, which is a whole category. Yeah, but I want it. Yes, I don't need it. So I just bought a whole bunch of new DVDs. I'm like, I don't do one damn trick from these 50 other DVDs I bought. I don't need it, but I want it because they make me want it. Right. And so again, really important. Okay, here's a good one. All right, I've got to talk to my butcher baker candlestick maker, my wife, my mistress, whoever it is. How do we handle that? Part of that goes back to bad salesmanship that Dan and I both talked about. And maybe we didn't set it up properly from the beginning. And I think that happens. That's lazy selling or that's the salesperson rushing to a prospect that doesn't have the ability to buy. And if they really, if you have to have all the people in the room, then you need to get all the people in the room. I mean, it's as simple as that. Why are you doing the presentations? Why are you exactly right? So we've talked chiropractors for years. In prepay, it's one thing if it's pay as you go. But if it's prepay and the end of a case presentation is you're going to commit to $14,000 worth of care. In most families, spouse, that's going to have to be discussed. So the only way to handle this objection in that environment is the spouse has to be there. They have to be. So it has to be a mandatory part of the report of findings presentation. And if they object to that, you can't have the presentation. There's no need to go forward. Here, let me give you the name of four or five other doctors. But not here. This is how we do it. And that gets to having enough lead flow. You know, all sorts of things. But he's a thousand percent right. If there is a person who is going to muck around and they are going to, I just had this happen with me and it's people who should know better, okay, selling me, attempting to sell me a very sophisticated and exotic financial instrument. And so they went through all the gyrations of getting this appointment, which is not easy. And then they want to do the call with us looking at go to meetings or something, you know, which I'm not going to do. So they've got to send me what I would see on the screen on paper before we get on the phone and all this. This is a very complicated purchase. It's an expensive purchase. You have to have a significant net worth to even be allowed to make the purchase in the first place. Now if you can't anticipate that the stall slash objection to this purchase is going to be, I have to speak to my CPA and my tax attorney and my financial advisor before I do this, you must be making the presentation for the first time. So why would you let that happen? Because all attempts to overcome it are going to be horribly pushy. There are no scripted attempts there. There are, but you try and turn it into a conditional purchase. You try and, you know, but really should have been handled before that should have been a conference call. Everybody should have been on the call. Right. And all the CPA can't get. Well, we're going to, I'm not going to finish the call. We'll rebook it for two weeks from now. Mr. Prospect, don't screw it up again. Or I'm not even going to let you rebook it at all. Right. I mean, you just have to have this handled properly. Yeah, absolutely.
